Goal:
Create a simple desktop Tic-Tac-Toe application using Python and Tkinter.

Original user request:
Would would be the ideal first successful orchestration

# Goal Definition: Simple Tic-Tac-Toe Desktop Application

## 1. Define the Objective

Create a simple desktop Tic-Tac-Toe application.

The application should allow two local players to play Tic-Tac-Toe on the same computer. The app should be intentionally small, readable, and easy to validate. Use the most straightforward implementation path for a coding orchestrator.

### Preferred stack

Use **Python 3 with Tkinter**.

Reasoning:

- Tkinter is included with most Python installations.
- No web server, build toolchain, package manager, or external UI framework is required.
- The app can be delivered as a single `.py` file.
- The UI and game logic are simple enough for direct inspection and testing.

### Core functionality

The application must include:

- A 3x3 Tic-Tac-Toe board.
- Two-player local play: `X` and `O`.
- Alternating turns.
- Clear indication of whose turn it is.
- Win detection for rows, columns, and diagonals.
- Draw detection when the board is full.
- A reset/new game button.
- Disabled moves on already occupied squares.
- A clear end-of-game message when a player wins or the game ends in a draw.

### Deliverable

Create a single executable Python script:

```text
tic_tac_toe.py

Context:
The application will allow two local players to play Tic-Tac-Toe on the same computer.

Plan:
- Set up the Tkinter environment: Initialize the Tkinter window and configure the basic layout for the Tic-Tac-Toe board.
- Implement game logic: Create functions to handle player turns, win detection, and game reset functionality.
- Add user interface elements: Design buttons for each square on the board, a reset button, and labels to indicate the current player's turn.
- Test the application: Conduct tests to ensure the game logic works correctly, including win and draw conditions.
- Package the application: Finalize the application into a single executable Python script named 'tic_tac_toe.py'.

Acceptance checks:
- Application runs without errors
- Game logic correctly detects wins and draws
- User interface is intuitive and responsive

Assumptions:
- Assumption: The application will be developed in a local environment using Python 3 and Tkinter.
- Assumption: No external libraries or frameworks will be used beyond Tkinter.
- Assumption: The application will be delivered as a single Python script.

Goal:
Build a browser-playable Vite + React + TypeScript HTML5 Canvas arcade game titled Pixel Burrow.

Original user request:
Build a browser-playable Vite + React + TypeScript HTML5 Canvas arcade game titled Pixel Burrow.

Required features and mechanics:
- Start screen with title, instructions, and Start button.
- Player turret in the lower play zone.
- Keyboard controls: Arrow keys or WASD to move, Space to fire, P to pause, R to restart.
- Player bullets fire upward with a cooldown.
- Segmented crawler enemy moves horizontally, reverses at edges/obstacles, and descends one row.
- Random obstacle field.
- Obstacles have health and can be damaged/destroyed by bullets.
- Destroyed crawler segments create new obstacles.
- Score, lives, level, and high score are displayed.
- High score persists in localStorage.
- Player loses a life on enemy collision.
- Game over occurs at zero lives.
- Restart works.
- Level advances after crawler is destroyed.
- Difficulty increases by level using crawler speed, segment count, and obstacle count.
- Optional generated Web Audio sounds and mute toggle may be added if simple.

Acceptance criteria:
- npm install, npm run dev, and npm run build complete successfully.
- TypeScript compiles without errors.
- Game is playable in a desktop browser without backend services.
- Canvas renders clearly at common desktop sizes.
- HUD visibly shows score, high score, lives, level, and pause state.
- Player movement is smooth and bounded to the playfield.
- Bullet collisions visibly affect obstacles and crawler segments.
- Crawler movement is readable and deterministic.
- Destroyed segments visibly produce obstacles.
- Game over and restart flow are clear.
- No copyrighted Centipede assets, names, sounds, sprites, or logos are used.
- README includes install, run, controls, gameplay, architecture, known limitations, and next steps.

Expected output: complete Vite + React + TypeScript project with organized source under src/, README.md, browser UI with start screen, game screen, HUD, pause, game over, restart, scoring, lives, levels, and persistent high score. No backend input is required.

Context:
The game will include various mechanics such as player controls, enemy behavior, scoring, and a user interface.

Plan:
- Set up project structure: Initialize a new Vite + React + TypeScript project and set up the directory structure.
- Implement game mechanics: Develop the core game mechanics including player controls, enemy behavior, and scoring.
- Create user interface: Design and implement the start screen, game HUD, and game over screens.
- Add audio features: Integrate optional Web Audio sounds and a mute toggle.
- Test and refine: Conduct thorough testing to ensure all acceptance criteria are met and refine the game based on feedback.

Acceptance checks:
- npm install, npm run dev, and npm run build complete successfully
- TypeScript compiles without errors
- Game is playable in a desktop browser without backend services
- Canvas renders clearly at common desktop sizes
- HUD visibly shows score, high score, lives, level, and pause state
- Player movement is smooth and bounded to the playfield
- Bullet collisions visibly affect obstacles and crawler segments
- Crawler movement is readable and deterministic
- Destroyed segments visibly produce obstacles
- Game over and restart flow are clear
- No copyrighted assets are used
- README includes install, run, controls, gameplay, architecture, known limitations, and next steps

Assumptions:
- Assumption: The project will be hosted on GitHub at the specified repo URL.
- Assumption: The game will be developed for desktop browsers only.
- Assumption: No backend services will be required for the game.
- Assumption: The game will use only original assets to avoid copyright issues.

Success criteria: Identify and rectify all errors in the orchestration script, ensuring successful deployment and operation of the web application.
